cover.title: Wireshark Introduction & __Setup__
cover.hook: See every packet on your network. Wireshark is the analyst's microscope.

slides.label: What is Wireshark?
slides.heading: Wireshark: The __Analyst's Microscope__

body.text: **Wireshark** is the world's most widely used free, open-source packet analyser. It captures live network traffic in real-time or reads saved `.pcap` files — showing you every single byte that crosses a network interface.

**Who uses Wireshark:** SOC analysts investigating incidents, penetration testers analysing traffic, network engineers troubleshooting, forensic investigators recovering evidence, and malware analysts understanding attack behaviour.

**Why it matters:** Every network attack — every exploit, every C2 beacon, every data exfiltration — leaves traces in network traffic. Wireshark lets you see those traces with complete detail at every layer of the OSI model.

slides.time: 20 min · wireshark.org/#educationalContent — Free Wireshark University

slides.label: Installation
slides.heading: Installing __Wireshark__: All Platforms

body.text:
# Ubuntu/Kali Linux:
sudo apt install wireshark

# Add user to wireshark group (capture without root):
sudo usermod -aG wireshark $USER
newgrp wireshark  # Apply group change immediately

# Verify installation:
wireshark --version

# CLI alternative (no GUI required):
sudo apt install tshark  # Terminal version of Wireshark
**Windows/Mac:** Download installer from wireshark.org/download.html — includes WinPcap/Npcap driver automatically. Must have a physical or virtual network interface card to capture live traffic.

slides.time: 15 min · wireshark.org/download.html

slides.label: Interface Overview
slides.heading: Wireshark Interface: __3 Panels__

body.text: **Packet List (top panel):** Every captured packet in chronological order. Columns: No., Time, Source, Destination, Protocol, Length, Info. Colour-coded by protocol type.
**Packet Details (middle panel):** Layer-by-layer breakdown of the selected packet. Expands each protocol header — Frame (L2) → IP (L3) → TCP (L4) → Application (L7). Click any field to inspect it.
**Hex Dump (bottom panel):** Raw bytes of the packet in hexadecimal. When you click a field in the details panel, its corresponding bytes highlight here. Essential for malware payload analysis.
**Filter bar** at the top turns green for valid filters, red for invalid. The **blue shark fin button** starts/stops live capture. The **Statistics menu** provides instant traffic profiling.

slides.time: 15 min · Explore each panel in your first capture

slides.label: First Packet Capture
slides.heading: Your First __Live Capture__: Step by Step

body.text: **Launch Wireshark** → you see a list of network interfaces with traffic sparklines. Select your active NIC (usually eth0 or wlan0 — the one with activity).
**Click the blue fin** to start capture. Open a browser and navigate to google.com. Stop capture after 30 seconds with the red square button.
**Observe the protocols:** You will see DNS (resolving google.com), TCP (connection establishment), TLS (encrypted HTTPS). Layer 1–7 all visible in one capture.
**Right-click any packet field** → 'Apply as Filter' → instantly filter to only packets matching that value. This is the most powerful Wireshark workflow shortcut.

slides.time: 20 min · Perform your first live capture right now

slides.label: What to Learn Today
slides.heading: Day 43 Checklist
steps: **Install Wireshark** and verify capture works — you can see packets flowing on your interface
**Start a 30-second capture** — browse to 3 different websites — stop and examine the packets
**Apply the `dns` filter** — see every domain your computer is resolving in real-time
**Expand a DNS packet** in the details panel — identify the query name and response IP
**Download a practice pcap** from malware-traffic-analysis.net — open and explore it
slides.time: Total Day 43 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes

resources.label: 📺 Video
resources.name: Chris Greer — Wireshark Tutorial for Beginners (YouTube)

slides.label: Action Plan
slides.heading: Your Hands-On __Task__ for Day 43
steps: **Capture** 30 seconds of traffic on your network — browse multiple sites during capture
**Apply** the `dns` filter — list every domain your computer contacted — note any unexpected ones
**Expand** one DNS packet fully — identify: query name, query type (A/AAAA/MX), response IP
**Download** a pcap from malware-traffic-analysis.net — open it and note the first 3 protocols in Protocol Hierarchy
slides.time: Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: The fastest way to learn Wireshark is practising with pre-made pcap files. malware-traffic-analysis.net has hundreds of real malware pcaps with answer keys for safe, legal analysis practice.
slides.sub: Save your first pcap file immediately after capture. File → Save As → `first_capture_YYYYMMDD.pcap`. Build a library of captures from different scenarios — normal traffic, your own Nmap scans, web browsing. Comparing normal traffic against malicious pcaps is how analysts develop intuition.
slides.mistake: Capturing on public networks, corporate networks, or any network you do not own without explicit written permission. Packet capture intercepts traffic from all devices on the network segment — not just your own. This is illegal in most jurisdictions even if you only analyse your own data. Always capture on your own lab network.

cover.title: Wireshark Filters & __Display Techniques__
cover.hook: 1,000 packets. Find the attack in 30 seconds. Wireshark filters are the key.

slides.label: Capture vs Display Filters
slides.method: WHAT — Two Types of Wireshark Filters
slides.heading: Two Filter Types: __Capture vs Display__

body.text: **Capture Filters (BPF):** Applied BEFORE and DURING capture. Use Berkeley Packet Filter syntax. Reduces noise at collection time — fewer packets stored. More efficient for targeted capture. Cannot be changed after capture starts.

# Capture filter examples (BPF syntax):
tcp port 80          # Only HTTP traffic
host 192.168.1.100   # Only traffic to/from this IP
not arp              # Exclude ARP broadcast noise
port 53              # Only DNS traffic

**Display Filters:** Applied AFTER capture to an existing file. Wireshark's own filter language — more powerful and flexible than BPF. Turn green when valid, red when invalid. Can be changed freely without losing captured data.

slides.time: 20 min · Wireshark University · Guru99 Wireshark tutorial

slides.label: Security Filter Cheat Sheet
slides.heading: Security Filter __Cheat Sheet__

body.text: tcp.flags.syn==1 && tcp.flags.ack==0  # SYN scan detection
icmp                                   # Ping sweep activity
http.request.uri contains "passwd"      # LFI attempt
http.request.uri contains "SELECT"      # SQL injection attempt
ftp                                    # Cleartext FTP (credentials!)
telnet                                 # Insecure remote access
dns.qry.name contains "evil"           # Suspicious DNS query
http.response.code == 404              # Directory brute-force
ip.src == 192.168.1.100 && tcp.flags.syn==1  # Specific IP scan

slides.time: 25 min · Apply each filter against a practice pcap

slides.label: Follow Stream
slides.heading: Follow Stream: __Reconstruct Conversations__

body.text: **Follow TCP Stream** is one of Wireshark's most powerful features. Right-click any TCP packet → 'Follow' → 'TCP Stream'. Wireshark reassembles the entire conversation and shows it in a readable window.
**HTTP sessions:** See full GET/POST requests, headers, and response bodies — including credentials submitted in web forms on unencrypted sites.
**FTP sessions:** See complete file transfer with the username and password in cleartext — FTP never encrypts credentials.
**SMTP emails:** Reconstruct full email content including attachments transmitted over unencrypted mail servers.
**Colour coding:** Client-to-server traffic shown in red. Server-to-client shown in blue. Instantly visualise the direction of each communication in the reconstructed stream.

slides.time: 20 min · Follow a TCP stream in your practice pcap

slides.label: Statistics Menu
slides.method: WHAT — Using Statistics for Rapid Triage
slides.heading: Statistics Menu: __3-Click Traffic Profile__

body.text: **Protocol Hierarchy:** Statistics → Protocol Hierarchy. Shows percentage breakdown of all protocols in the capture. Unexpected high-volume protocols (like DNS or ICMP when you expect HTTP) = immediate red flag.
**Conversations:** Statistics → Conversations. Top talking IP pairs, bytes transferred, packet counts. Identifies the noisiest hosts — attackers and C2 beacons appear here.
**I/O Graph:** Statistics → I/O Graph. Traffic volume over time — attack spikes, beaconing intervals, and DDoS patterns visible instantly.
**Endpoints:** Statistics → Endpoints. All unique IPs and their traffic volume. External IPs your internal hosts contacted — identify unexpected cloud infrastructure or C2 servers.

slides.time: 20 min · Apply all 4 statistics views to a malware pcap

slides.label: What to Learn Today
slides.heading: Day 44 Checklist
steps: Apply the **SYN scan filter** — `tcp.flags.syn==1 && tcp.flags.ack==0` — understand what you see
Build your **personal filter library** — save 10 security filters using Analyze → Display Filters
Use **Follow TCP Stream** on an HTTP connection — identify what data was transmitted
Run all **4 Statistics views** on a malware pcap — write a 3-sentence traffic profile
Download a CTF pcap from malware-traffic-analysis.net and **write a 5-sentence incident summary**
slides.time: Total Day 44 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes

resources.label: 📖 Reference
resources.name: Wireshark Display Filter Reference — Complete List

slides.label: Action Plan
slides.heading: Your Hands-On __Task__ for Day 44
steps: **Download** a malware pcap from malware-traffic-analysis.net — open in Wireshark
**Apply** these 3 filters in sequence: `http` → `dns` → `tcp.flags.syn==1` — document findings each time
**Follow a TCP stream** — reconstruct the full HTTP conversation and describe what happened
**Write** a 5-sentence incident summary: what protocol, what attack type, source IP, destination IP, severity
slides.time: Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: Build a personal filter library in Wireshark: Analyze → Display Filters → save your security filters. You will use the same 10–15 filters 90% of the time in real investigations.
slides.sub: Enable Coloring Rules immediately (View → Coloring Rules). Different protocol conversations get different colours — HTTP in green, TCP SYN in red, DNS in blue. Colour-coded packets let your eyes immediately spot anomalies before you apply a single filter. It is the fastest triage technique in Wireshark.
slides.mistake: Applying display filters without first running Protocol Hierarchy and Conversations statistics. Jumping straight to filtering means you might miss the actual attack — which could be in a protocol you did not think to filter for. Always start with 2 minutes of statistics to understand what is IN the capture before filtering.

cover.title: TCP/IP Deep Dive with __Wireshark__
cover.hook: See TCP's 3-way handshake in real packets. Theory becomes reality.

slides.label: 3-Way Handshake in Wireshark
slides.method: WHAT — Seeing TCP Handshake in Packets
slides.heading: 3-Way Handshake: __Theory Becomes Reality__

body.text: The TCP 3-way handshake you learned in Day 2 is now visible as real packets. Filter: `tcp && ip.addr==target`. You will see the exact SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK sequence with timestamps.

# Filter to see handshake for specific connection:
tcp && ip.addr==192.168.1.1

# In the packet list:
# Packet 1: SYN      flags=0x002  Client → Server
# Packet 2: SYN-ACK  flags=0x012  Server → Client
# Packet 3: ACK      flags=0x010  Client → Server
# Packet 4+: Data exchange begins
**The Delta Time column** shows latency between packets. Add it via: View → Column Preferences → + → Delta Time Displayed. High latency between SYN and SYN-ACK reveals slow or distant servers.

slides.time: 25 min · Wireshark University · Guru99 TCP analysis

slides.label: TCP Flags in Packets
slides.heading: TCP Flags: __6 Flags__, Many Attack Patterns

body.text:
# TCP Flag values (hexadecimal):
SYN   = 0x002  # Initiate connection
ACK   = 0x010  # Acknowledge
FIN   = 0x001  # Graceful close
RST   = 0x004  # Abrupt reset
PSH   = 0x008  # Push data immediately
URG   = 0x020  # Urgent data

# Wireshark filter for each:
tcp.flags == 0x002           # Pure SYN (new connection or scan)
tcp.flags == 0x012           # SYN-ACK (open port response)
tcp.flags.reset == 1         # All RST packets (resets)
tcp.flags == 0x029           # URG+SYN+ACK (unusual — investigate)

**Attack detection:** A single source IP sending SYN packets to hundreds of different ports = **port scan**. Many SYN packets with no ACK completions = **SYN flood DoS**. Unexpected RST packets in established sessions = **TCP session injection**.

slides.time: 20 min · Apply each flag filter against your practice pcap

slides.label: Spotting Port Scans
slides.method: HOW — Identifying Nmap Scans in Wireshark
slides.heading: Detecting __Port Scans__ in Wireshark

body.text:
# Filter to detect SYN scan from single IP:
tcp.flags.syn==1 && tcp.flags.ack==0 && ip.src==192.168.1.100
# Shows: IP sending SYN to many ports (Nmap -sS)

# See the responses — open vs closed ports:
tcp.flags.syn==1 && tcp.flags.ack==1  # SYN-ACK = port OPEN
tcp.flags.reset==1                    # RST = port CLOSED

# Connect scan detection (full 3-way handshake):
tcp.flags == 0x002  # then filter by source IP
# Nmap -sT = completes handshake then sends RST

**Expert tip:** Run `nmap -sS localhost` in one terminal while Wireshark is capturing in another. Watch your own scan appear in real-time — you will see exactly what a victim's IDS sees when they are being scanned.

slides.time: 25 min · Capture your own Nmap scan and analyse it

slides.label: HTTP in Wireshark
slides.heading: HTTP Analysis: __Web Attacks Visible__

body.text:
# HTTP request filtering:
http.request              # All HTTP requests
http.request.method=="GET"  # Only GET requests
http.request.method=="POST" # Form submissions, logins
http.request.uri contains "/admin"  # Admin panel access

# Response filtering:
http.response.code == 200  # Successful responses
http.response.code == 404  # Not found (brute force!)
http.response.code == 403  # Forbidden (access control)

# Credential exposure detection:
http contains "password"   # Password in cleartext
http contains "login"      # Login form submissions
# Follow TCP Stream on any POST → may see credentials!

slides.time: 20 min · Analyse HTTP traffic in a practice pcap

slides.label: What to Learn Today
slides.heading: Day 45 Checklist
steps: Identify the **3-way handshake** (SYN/SYN-ACK/ACK) in a live capture — expand each packet
Capture your **own Nmap -sS scan** on localhost and observe it in Wireshark simultaneously
Use **`tcp.flags.syn==1 && tcp.flags.ack==0`** — count how many ports were scanned
Filter **`http.response.code == 404`** — identify any directory brute-force activity
Use **Expert Information** (Analyze menu) — identify all retransmissions and resets
slides.time: Total Day 45 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes

resources.label: 📺 Video
resources.name: Chris Greer — TCP Analysis with Wireshark (YouTube)

slides.label: Action Plan
slides.heading: Your Hands-On __Task__ for Day 45
steps: **Run** `nmap -sS localhost` in one terminal while Wireshark is capturing on loopback interface
**Apply** `tcp.flags.syn==1 && tcp.flags.ack==0` — count SYN packets from your scan
**Compare** SYN-ACK responses (open ports) vs RST responses (closed ports) — list open ports found
**Use** Expert Information (Analyze → Expert Information) — note all anomalies flagged automatically
slides.time: Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: Wireshark's Expert Information automatically flags anomalies — retransmissions, resets, malformed packets. Start every pcap analysis there. It is your automated first-pass triage.
slides.sub: The most valuable skill in TCP analysis is knowing what NORMAL looks like. Capture your baseline traffic now — an hour of normal browsing, normal downloads, normal DNS. Save it. Every future investigation starts by comparing suspicious traffic against this baseline. Anomalies only exist relative to normal.
slides.mistake: Ignoring TCP RST storms. A flood of RST packets in a capture is almost always either malicious (TCP session injection, port scan, DoS) or severely misconfigured. RSTs are the network equivalent of a slammed phone — something went wrong. Every RST in an investigation warrants examination of the source, destination, and timing.

cover.title: DNS Analysis & __DNS Attack Detection__
cover.hook: DNS is the most abused protocol for attacks and the most overlooked in defence.

slides.label: DNS in Wireshark
slides.heading: DNS in Wireshark: __Every Query Visible__

body.text: **Filter: `dns`** — instantly shows every DNS query and response in the capture. DNS uses UDP port 53 for most queries (small packets) and TCP port 53 for large responses and zone transfers.

# DNS display filters:
dns                           # All DNS traffic
dns.flags.response == 0       # Only queries (QR=0)
dns.flags.response == 1       # Only responses (QR=1)
dns.qry.type == 1             # A record queries only
dns.qry.type == 16            # TXT record queries
dns.flags.rcode == 3          # NXDomain (failed lookups)
**Normal DNS behaviour:** UDP, small packets (< 512 bytes), fast response times (< 100ms), simple domain names, mostly A and AAAA record types. Any deviation from this pattern warrants investigation.

slides.time: 20 min · Wireshark University · Guru99 DNS analysis tutorial

slides.label: DNS Tunneling
slides.heading: DNS Tunneling: __Data Exfiltration via DNS__

body.text: **DNS tunneling** encodes data inside DNS queries — allowing attackers to exfiltrate sensitive information or establish C2 communications even through firewalls that block all other outbound traffic. DNS port 53 is almost never blocked.

# Example DNS tunnel query:
d2hhdGV2ZXJkYXRh.evil-c2-domain.com
# The subdomain is base64-encoded stolen data:
# d2hhdGV2ZXJkYXRh = base64('whateverdata')
**Sign 1:** Unusually long subdomains (normal = < 20 chars, tunneling = 50+ chars)
**Sign 2:** High volume of DNS queries — tunneling tools send hundreds per minute
**Sign 3:** High rate of TXT record queries — used for bidirectional tunnel communication
**Sign 4:** Queries to uncommon TLDs or newly registered domains

slides.time: 25 min · Download a DNS tunneling pcap and analyse

slides.label: Detecting DNS Tunneling
slides.method: HOW — Identifying DNS Tunneling in Wireshark
slides.heading: DNS Tunnel __Detection__ Filters

body.text:
# Detect unusually long domain names (tunneling sign):
dns.qry.name.len > 52

# High volume of TXT queries (bidirectional tunnel):
dns.qry.type == 16  # TXT record type

# Failed lookups for random-looking domains (DGA/tunnel):
dns.flags.rcode == 3  # NXDomain responses

# Statistics → DNS shows query type distribution:
# Normal: mostly A/AAAA records
# Tunneling: unusually high TXT or abnormal types

**Tools that create DNS tunnels:** iodine, dnscat2, dns2tcp. If you see base64-looking strings in subdomain labels combined with high query volume to a single domain — that is textbook DNS tunneling. Extract the subdomains, base64 decode them, and see the exfiltrated data.

slides.time: 20 min · Apply tunnel detection filters to a sample pcap

slides.label: DGA & Passive DNS
slides.heading: DGA Domains & __Passive DNS__ Intelligence

body.text: **DGA (Domain Generation Algorithm):** Malware generates hundreds of pseudo-random domain names daily. Only one is actually registered — the C2 server. All others fail (NXDomain). In Wireshark, DGA creates a flood of NXDomain responses.

# DGA detection in Wireshark:
dns.flags.rcode == 3  # Filter NXDomain failures
# Look for: many different random-looking domains
# like: xk3mp2v.com, b8jqz9n.net, mq7rk9p.org
# All from the same infected internal host

**Passive DNS** tracks historical domain→IP mappings over time. Tools like SecurityTrails, RiskIQ, and VirusTotal passive DNS reveal if a domain has changed IPs rapidly (fast-flux) — a C2 server evasion technique. Always check suspicious domains against passive DNS during an investigation.

slides.time: 20 min · Search a suspicious domain in VirusTotal passive DNS

slides.label: What to Learn Today
slides.heading: Day 46 Checklist
steps: Apply `dns.qry.name.len > 52` to a tunneling pcap — count unusually long domain names
Identify **NXDomain floods** with `dns.flags.rcode == 3` — potential DGA malware
Use **Statistics → DNS** to see the query type distribution — spot abnormal TXT record volume
Download a DNS tunneling pcap from PacketLife.net or malware-traffic-analysis.net — identify the tunnel domain
Understand why **blocking DNS to unauthorised servers** at the firewall is a critical control
slides.time: Total Day 46 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes

resources.label: 🔍 Intel
resources.name: VirusTotal — Passive DNS and Domain Intelligence

slides.label: Action Plan
slides.heading: Your Hands-On __Task__ for Day 46
steps: **Download** a DNS tunneling pcap from PacketLife.net or malware-traffic-analysis.net
**Apply** `dns.qry.name.len > 40` — list all unusually long domain names found
**Count** unique domains queried — compare the ratio of NXDomain vs successful lookups
**Compare** to a normal DNS capture from yesterday — document 3 specific differences
slides.time: Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: DNS is the most overlooked protocol in security monitoring. Adding a simple DNS anomaly detection rule to your SIEM catches 30% of malware C2 traffic that every other control misses.
slides.sub: The most powerful DNS security control is also the simplest: block outbound DNS to any server except your authorised resolvers at the firewall. Force all DNS through your monitoring resolver. This single firewall rule eliminates DNS tunneling and ensures every DNS query is visible in your logs.
slides.mistake: Overlooking DNS during traffic analysis because 'it is just DNS.' DNS carries no payload data in normal use — which makes any anomaly in DNS volume, query types, or domain name length immediately suspicious. An attacker who can exfiltrate data via DNS needs no other network path. Monitor DNS like it is your most critical protocol.

cover.title: HTTP/HTTPS Analysis & __Web Attack Detection__
cover.hook: Web traffic is 80% of enterprise network. Learn to read and analyse it.

slides.label: HTTP in Wireshark
slides.heading: HTTP Structure: __Every Request Visible__

body.text:
# HTTP display filters:
http                          # All HTTP traffic
http.request                  # Requests only
http.response                 # Responses only
http.request.method == "POST" # Form submissions, logins
http.request.method == "GET"  # Page/resource requests
http.host == "target.com"     # Specific domain only

**File extraction from pcap:** Wireshark can extract files transferred over HTTP. File → Export Objects → HTTP. This extracts images, documents, executables, and scripts downloaded by the victim — critical for malware analysis.
**HTTP request structure visible in details panel:** Method, URI, HTTP version, all headers (User-Agent, Host, Cookie, Authorization), and the request body (form data, JSON payloads).

slides.time: 20 min · Wireshark University · Guru99 Wireshark HTTP guide

slides.label: Web Attack Detection
slides.method: WHAT — Detecting Attacks in HTTP Traffic
slides.heading: Web Attacks: __Visible in HTTP Traffic__

body.text:
# SQL Injection attempts:
http.request.uri contains "SELECT"
http.request.uri contains "%27"   # URL-encoded quote (')
http.request.uri contains "UNION%20SELECT"

# Directory brute force (mass 404s):
http.response.code == 404
# Combined with high request rate from single IP

# Path traversal (LFI) attempts:
http.request.uri contains "../"
http.request.uri contains "etc/passwd"

# Credential submission in cleartext:
http.request.method == "POST"
# Follow TCP Stream → search for 'password=' in body

# Command injection in parameters:
http.request.uri contains ";"
http.request.uri contains "|"

slides.time: 25 min · Apply each filter against a web attack pcap

slides.label: HTTPS Decryption
slides.method: HOW — Decrypting Your Own HTTPS Traffic
slides.heading: TLS Decryption: __Decrypt Your Own HTTPS__

body.text: **SSLKEYLOGFILE technique:** Modern browsers can log TLS session keys to a file as connections are made. Wireshark reads this file to decrypt the traffic — revealing the plaintext even though it was encrypted on the wire.

# Step 1: Set environment variable before opening browser:
export SSLKEYLOGFILE=/tmp/sslkeys.log   # Linux/Mac
# Windows: System Properties → Environment Variables

# Step 2: Open browser AFTER setting the variable
# Browse to target sites — keys are logged automatically
# Step 3: In Wireshark:
# Edit → Preferences → Protocols → TLS
# → (Pre)-Master-Secret log filename: /tmp/sslkeys.log

**Use cases:** Debugging your own web applications, malware analysis in a controlled lab environment, understanding what your own browser sends in HTTPS requests. **Only decrypt traffic you own or have explicit permission to decrypt.**

slides.time: 25 min · Decrypt your own browser HTTPS traffic

slides.label: HTTP Export Objects
slides.method: HOW — Extracting Files from HTTP Traffic
slides.heading: Export Objects: __Extract Files from PCAPs__

body.text: **File → Export Objects → HTTP** extracts all files transferred over HTTP in the capture. This is one of the most important forensic techniques for malware analysis — you can recover the exact executable or document that infected a victim.
Open a malware pcap → File → Export Objects → HTTP → you see a list of all downloaded files
Note the filenames, content types, and sizes — executables (.exe, .dll) and scripts (.vbs, .ps1) are immediately suspicious
Save suspicious files → upload their SHA256 hash to VirusTotal → confirm malware family
The file you extracted IS the malware that infected the victim — you now have your primary forensic artefact

slides.time: 20 min · Extract and hash files from a malware pcap

slides.label: What to Learn Today
slides.heading: Day 47 Checklist
steps: Apply **SQL injection detection filters** — identify any URI containing SELECT, UNION, or URL-encoded quotes
Filter for **mass 404 responses** from a single IP — confirm directory brute-force activity
Use **File → Export Objects → HTTP** to extract files from a malware pcap — hash and check on VirusTotal
Set **SSLKEYLOGFILE** and decrypt your own browser HTTPS traffic — see what github.com sends in plaintext
Follow a **POST request TCP stream** — identify any credentials or sensitive data in the request body
slides.time: Total Day 47 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes

resources.name: Wireshark HTTP Wiki — HTTP Analysis Guide

resources.label: 🏋️ Labs
resources.name: PortSwigger Web Academy — Free HTTP Security Labs

resources.label: 📖 Guide
resources.name: OWASP Web Security Testing Guide — Traffic Analysis

slides.label: Action Plan
slides.heading: Your Hands-On __Task__ for Day 47
steps: **Set** SSLKEYLOGFILE environment variable — open Firefox — browse to github.com — capture in Wireshark
**Load** the key log file in Wireshark TLS settings — confirm you can now read decrypted HTTPS

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: The SSLKEYLOGFILE technique is used by analysts to investigate encrypted malware C2 traffic in controlled lab environments. Understanding it also means understanding how enterprise SSL inspection appliances work.
slides.sub: Mass 404 errors from a single source IP in a short time window is one of the clearest signatures of directory brute-forcing. Rate threshold: anything above 50 requests per minute from a single IP to a single server generating mostly 404s should trigger an alert. Document this threshold when you write Suricata rules in Day 49.
slides.mistake: Only decrypting your own traffic. The SSLKEYLOGFILE method requires access to the browser generating the traffic — which means it only works for traffic YOU generate or traffic from a controlled lab environment. In real incident response, you cannot retroactively decrypt enterprise HTTPS traffic without a network SSL inspection appliance that was capturing keys at the time.

cover.title: tcpdump: __Command-Line Packet Analysis__
cover.hook: No GUI? No problem. tcpdump is the analyst's terminal sniffer.

slides.label: tcpdump Basics
slides.heading: tcpdump: __CLI Packet Capture__

body.text:
# Basic capture on interface:
sudo tcpdump -i eth0

# Essential flags:
tcpdump -i eth0   # Specific interface
tcpdump -n        # Don't resolve IPs to hostnames (faster)
tcpdump -v        # Verbose — more packet detail
tcpdump -vv       # Very verbose — full packet decode
tcpdump -c 100    # Capture only 100 packets then stop

# Write to file (for Wireshark analysis later):
sudo tcpdump -i eth0 -w /tmp/capture.pcap

# Read from file:
tcpdump -r /tmp/capture.pcap
**Pre-installed on:** every Linux and macOS system. No installation required. This is why it is the answer when interviewers ask 'how would you capture traffic on a remote server with no GUI?'

slides.time: 20 min · danielmiessler.com/study/tcpdump — definitive guide

slides.label: Essential tcpdump Filters
slides.heading: tcpdump Filters: __BPF Syntax Mastery__

body.text:
# Filter by port:
tcpdump port 80                    # HTTP
tcpdump port 443                   # HTTPS
tcpdump port 22                    # SSH

# Filter by host:
tcpdump host 192.168.1.100         # To/from specific IP
tcpdump src 192.168.1.100          # From this IP only
tcpdump dst 8.8.8.8                # To this IP only

# Filter by protocol:
tcpdump tcp                        # TCP only
tcpdump udp                        # UDP only
tcpdump arp                        # ARP only
tcpdump icmp                       # ICMP/ping only

# Combine filters with boolean operators:
tcpdump -i eth0 port 443 and host 8.8.8.8
tcpdump -i any 'tcp dst port 22 and not src 192.168.1.1'

slides.time: 25 min · Practice each filter combination in your terminal

slides.label: Security Monitoring One-Liners
slides.heading: Security One-Liners: __Real SOC Commands__

body.text:
# Monitor SSH brute-force in real-time:
sudo tcpdump -i any -n \
  'tcp dst port 22 and tcp[tcpflags] & tcp-syn != 0'

# Watch all DNS queries live:
sudo tcpdump -i any -n port 53

# Monitor ARP activity (detect ARP spoofing):
sudo tcpdump -i eth0 arp

# Capture HTTP traffic with full packet content:
sudo tcpdump -i any -A port 80
# -A prints packet in ASCII — reveals cleartext data

# Capture TCP SYN packets only (new connections):
sudo tcpdump -i any \
  'tcp[tcpflags] & tcp-syn != 0 and tcp[tcpflags] & tcp-ack == 0'

slides.time: 25 min · Run each one-liner on your Linux system

slides.label: tcpdump → Wireshark Workflow
slides.heading: Remote Capture Workflow: __The Enterprise Standard__

body.text: **SSH into remote server** where the traffic is occurring — a web server, a compromised host, or a network chokepoint.
**Start tcpdump capture:** `sudo tcpdump -i eth0 -w /tmp/incident_$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M).pcap -c 10000`
**Copy pcap to your local machine:** `scp user@server:/tmp/incident_*.pcap ~/investigations/`

**Open in Wireshark** on your local machine with full GUI capabilities — apply all filters, follow streams, export objects. This is how 90% of enterprise incident response traffic analysis is performed.

This documentation becomes part of the chain of custody in any investigation with legal implications. The command, timestamp, and who ran it must all be recorded.
slides.mistake: Running tcpdump without the `-n` flag on production systems. Without `-n`, tcpdump performs reverse DNS lookups for every IP — generating massive DNS traffic that can overwhelm DNS servers, alter the packet timing in your capture, and appear suspicious in your own logs. Always use `-n` on production captures unless hostname resolution is specifically needed.

cover.title: Suricata IDS/IPS __Setup & Rules__
cover.hook: Automate threat detection. Suricata watches your network 24/7.

slides.label: What is Suricata?
slides.heading: Suricata: __Free Enterprise IDS/IPS__

body.text: **Suricata** is a free, open-source, high-performance Intrusion Detection System (IDS), Intrusion Prevention System (IPS), and Network Security Monitor (NSM). It inspects all network packets in real-time against a library of threat signatures.

**Enterprise-grade:** Processes 10Gbps+ traffic on modern hardware. Used in commercial SOC environments, cloud security platforms, and network security appliances worldwide. The same engine that powers pfSense IDS.
**Three operating modes:** IDS (alert only, out-of-band), IPS (inline, blocks traffic), NSM (network security monitor, full logging). Most home labs start with IDS mode — alerting without blocking.

slides.time: 20 min · youtube.com — Suricata IDS/IPS Tutorial

slides.label: Installation & Setup
slides.heading: Suricata __Installation__ and Configuration

body.text:
# Install Suricata:
sudo apt install suricata

# Update to Emerging Threats ruleset:
sudo suricata-update

# Test configuration:
suricata -T -c /etc/suricata/suricata.yaml
# Output should end with: 'Configuration provided was good.'

# Start Suricata on live interface:
sudo systemctl start suricata
sudo systemctl enable suricata  # Start on boot

# Monitor live alerts:
sudo tail -f /var/log/suricata/fast.log

# Run against existing pcap file:
sudo suricata -r malware.pcap \
  -c /etc/suricata/suricata.yaml \
  -l /tmp/suricata-logs/
# Then check: cat /tmp/suricata-logs/fast.log

slides.time: 25 min · Install and run against a malware pcap

slides.label: Suricata Rule Anatomy
slides.heading: Rule Anatomy: __Every Field Explained__

body.text: **alert** — action (alert/drop/reject/pass). IDS mode uses alert only.
**tcp any any → $HOME_NET 22** — protocol, source IP, source port, direction, dest IP, dest port
**msg:** — human-readable alert name shown in logs
**threshold:** — prevents alert storm — only fire after 5 attempts in 60 seconds
**sid:** — unique rule identifier. Custom rules use sid > 9000000

slides.time: 25 min · Write 3 custom rules for common attack patterns

slides.label: Emerging Threats Rules
slides.heading: Emerging Threats: __30,000+ Free Rules__

body.text: **Emerging Threats (ET)** by Proofpoint provides free, daily-updated Suricata rules covering known malware families, exploit kits, C2 communication, and attack techniques. Over 30,000 rules updated every 24 hours.

# Update ET rules automatically:
sudo suricata-update

# List available rule sources:
sudo suricata-update list-sources

# Enable additional sources:
sudo suricata-update enable-source et/open
sudo suricata-update enable-source ptresearch/attackdetection

**EVE JSON log format** is what SIEMs ingest. Every Suricata alert is logged to `/var/log/suricata/eve.json` as structured JSON — timestamp, alert signature, source/destination IPs and ports, protocol, packet payload. Feed this directly into Elasticsearch or Splunk for enterprise detection.

slides.time: 20 min · Run suricata-update and review rule categories

slides.label: What to Learn Today
slides.heading: Day 49 Checklist
steps: **Install Suricata** and run `suricata -T` to validate your configuration
Run `sudo suricata-update` and count how many rules were downloaded
Run Suricata against a **malware pcap** — count alerts in fast.log — identify top rule names
**Write one custom rule** — detect SSH brute force (5+ connections from same IP in 60 seconds)
Understand **EVE JSON format** — open eve.json and identify the key fields for SIEM integration
slides.time: Total Day 49 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes

slides.label: Action Plan
slides.heading: Your Hands-On __Task__ for Day 49
steps: **Install** Suricata: `sudo apt install suricata` → run `suricata-update` → test with `-T` flag
**Run** against a malware pcap: `sudo suricata -r malware.pcap -l /tmp/logs/` — check fast.log
**Count** alerts: `wc -l /tmp/logs/fast.log` — identify the top 3 rule names that fired
**Write** a custom SSH brute-force rule — test it by replaying a brute-force pcap
slides.time: Hands-On Task: 75 – 90 minutes

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: Writing custom Suricata rules is a valued, rare skill — it is how SOC teams tune detection to their specific environment. Start by modifying existing rules before writing from scratch.
slides.sub: The most important Suricata tuning skill is the threshold option. Without it, a single attacker sending 10,000 SYN packets generates 10,000 alerts — burying analysts in noise. Threshold-based rules (fire only after N events in X seconds) is what makes IDS practical in real environments. Never deploy a detection rule without considering its alert volume.
slides.mistake: Enabling too many rules without tuning. A default Suricata deployment with all ET rules enabled will fire thousands of alerts per hour in any real network — most of them false positives. Start with a minimal ruleset, tune for your network baseline, then add rules incrementally. Alert fatigue is the most common reason IDS deployments fail in enterprise.

cover.title: Analysing __Malware Traffic__ Patterns
cover.hook: Day 50 of 90! Halfway there. Let us analyse real malware network traffic.

slides.label: C2 Traffic Patterns
slides.heading: C2 Traffic: __How Malware Phones Home__

body.text: **Command & Control (C2)** traffic is how malware communicates with its operator after infecting a victim. Identifying C2 traffic is the core skill of malware traffic analysis — find the C2 and you have identified the threat.
**Periodic beaconing:** C2 malware checks in every N seconds. Regular, clockwork-like connection intervals — not human browsing patterns. Statistics → I/O Graph reveals regular spikes.
**Unusual User-Agents:** Malware often sends fake User-Agent strings. Common: `Mozilla/4.0 (compatible)`, `curl/7.68.0`, random strings, or empty UA fields. Filter: `http.user_agent`.
**Non-standard ports:** HTTP traffic on port 8080, 8443, or completely random ports. HTTPS on non-443 ports.
**Encoded/encrypted payloads:** Base64 in HTTP bodies, random-looking binary data in TCP streams.

slides.time: 25 min · malware-traffic-analysis.net — free practice pcaps

slides.label: Beaconing Detection
slides.heading: Beaconing: __Find the Heartbeat__

body.text: **Beaconing detection in Wireshark:** Statistics → I/O Graph. Regular spikes at fixed intervals (every 30s, 60s, 5min) are the hallmark of C2 malware beaconing. Human browsing is irregular — malware is clockwork-precise.

# Wireshark filters for beaconing investigation:
ip.addr == suspected_c2_ip      # All traffic to C2
http.user_agent                  # Check all UA strings
tcp.stream eq N                  # Follow specific stream

**Statistics → Conversations** for beaconing: Look for an internal host with dozens or hundreds of short connections to a single external IP, all at regular time intervals. The external IP is your C2 server.

**Beacon jitter:** Sophisticated malware adds random jitter to beacon intervals to evade exact-interval detection. Instead of every 60 seconds, it beacons every 57–63 seconds. Look for near-regular intervals, not just exact ones.

slides.time: 25 min · Identify beaconing in a malware pcap

slides.label: DGA Domains
slides.heading: DGA: __Random Domains__, Real Malware

body.text: **Domain Generation Algorithms (DGA)** create hundreds of pseudo-random domain names daily. The malware author registers only one — the rest generate NXDomain failures. This makes C2 infrastructure extremely resilient — block one domain and the malware tries the next 200.

# DGA detection in Wireshark:
dns.flags.rcode == 3  # Filter NXDomain failures
# Signs:
# - Many different random-looking domains from same host
# - Like: xk3mp2v.com, b8jqz9n.net, mq7rk9p.org
# - All from ONE infected internal IP
# - Eventually ONE domain resolves successfully = C2

**DGA families:** CryptoLocker, Conficker, Zeus, Dridex all use DGA. Threat intelligence feeds contain known DGA patterns. When you see a flood of NXDomain responses from a single internal host — you have found your patient zero.

slides.time: 20 min · Identify DGA activity in a malware pcap

slides.label: Lateral Movement
slides.heading: Lateral Movement: __Inside the Network__

body.text: After initial compromise, malware spreads internally. **Lateral movement in Wireshark** involves unusual internal-to-internal traffic patterns that do not match normal business communication.

# Lateral movement indicators:
smb                         # SMB traffic between internal hosts
tcp.port == 445             # SMB (EternalBlue territory)
tcp.port == 5985            # WinRM (PowerShell remoting)
tcp.port == 3389            # RDP (remote desktop)
tcp.port == 135             # WMI (Windows management)

**Key question:** Have these two internal hosts ever communicated before? A workstation connecting to 50 other internal servers via SMB within 10 minutes = **WannaCry-style worm propagation**. Normal workstations only talk to a few servers. Unusual internal traffic volumes = immediate investigation priority.

slides.time: 20 min · Identify lateral movement in a malware pcap

slides.label: What to Learn Today
slides.heading: Day 50 Checklist — __Halfway There!__
steps: Download **3 malware pcaps** from malware-traffic-analysis.net — identify the C2 domain in each
Use **Statistics → I/O Graph** to spot beaconing — identify the beacon interval
Filter **`dns.flags.rcode == 3`** — count NXDomain failures — identify potential DGA activity
Filter **SMB and RDP** traffic — identify any internal lateral movement patterns
Write a **Suricata rule** that would detect the beaconing pattern you identified

cover.title: Zeek (Bro) __Network Security Monitor__
cover.hook: Zeek turns raw packets into structured security logs. Enterprise-level monitoring.

slides.label: What is Zeek?
slides.heading: Zeek: Packets In, __Structured Logs Out__

body.text: **Zeek** (formerly Bro) is a powerful Network Security Monitor that processes raw packets and generates high-level, structured log files automatically. Rather than alerting on specific signatures, Zeek creates a complete audit trail of all network activity.

**The key difference from Suricata:** Suricata fires alerts on known bad patterns. Zeek creates comprehensive logs of ALL activity — letting analysts hunt for unknown threats that no signature covers yet.

**Zeek automatically generates:** conn.log (every connection), dns.log (all DNS), http.log (all URLs), ssl.log (all TLS certificates), files.log (all file transfers), weird.log (protocol anomalies) — without any configuration.

slides.time: 20 min · zeek.org/documentation

slides.label: Installation & Running Zeek
slides.heading: Zeek __Setup__: Analyse Any PCAP

body.text:
# Install Zeek:
sudo apt install zeek

# Verify installation:
zeek --version

# Analyse a pcap file (no live capture needed):
zeek -r malware.pcap
# Generates log files in current directory:
# conn.log dns.log http.log ssl.log files.log weird.log

# Run on live interface (requires root):
sudo zeekctl deploy  # Uses /etc/zeek/node.cfg config

# Or direct interface capture:
sudo zeek -i eth0

# View logs immediately:
cat conn.log | head -5
cat dns.log | head -5
cat http.log | head -5

slides.time: 25 min · Run Zeek against a malware pcap now

slides.label: Key Log Files
slides.heading: Zeek Logs: __Complete Network Audit Trail__

body.text:
# conn.log — every TCP/UDP connection:
# Fields: ts, uid, id.orig_h, id.resp_h, id.resp_p,
#         proto, duration, orig_bytes, resp_bytes

# Top talkers by bytes transferred:
cat conn.log | zeek-cut id.orig_h id.resp_h resp_bytes \
  | sort -k3 -rn | head -10

# http.log — every HTTP request:
# Fields: ts, uid, id.orig_h, host, uri, method,
#         status_code, user_agent, resp_mime_types

# Extract all URLs visited:
cat http.log | zeek-cut host uri | head -20

# dns.log — every DNS query:
# Extract unique domains queried:
cat dns.log | zeek-cut query qtype_name answers \
  | sort | uniq | head -20

# weird.log — protocol anomalies:
# If this log has content = investigate immediately

slides.time: 25 min · Analyse all log files from your malware pcap

slides.label: Zeek vs Wireshark
slides.method: WHAT — When to Use Each Tool
slides.heading: Zeek vs Wireshark: __Different Jobs__

body.text: **Wireshark:** Packet-level detail. GUI-based. Best for deep protocol analysis, following individual streams, decrypting traffic, and extracting specific artefacts. Difficult to scale to large captures.

**Zeek:** Session-level summary logs. CLI-based. Best for large-scale traffic analysis, hunting across millions of events, long-term logging, and integration with SIEMs. Cannot show individual packet bytes.

**Professional workflow:** Zeek for initial triage and hunting → Wireshark for deep-dive investigation of specific sessions. A Zeek conn.log entry includes a `uid` field — use it with `zeek-cut` to extract the specific packets for Wireshark analysis.

**Zeek + Elastic Stack = open-source SIEM.** Many organisations run this combination instead of paid SIEM solutions — saving tens of thousands annually while maintaining enterprise-grade detection capability.

slides.time: 15 min · Compare Zeek and Wireshark output on the same pcap

slides.label: What to Learn Today
slides.heading: Day 51 Checklist

Use **`zeek-cut`** to extract top talkers from conn.log by bytes transferred
Extract all **unique DNS queries** from dns.log — compare to the Wireshark DNS filter from Day 46
Check **weird.log** — if it has any entries, investigate every single one

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: Zeek + Elastic Stack is a powerful open-source SIEM substitute. Many organisations run this stack instead of paid SIEMs. Knowing Zeek makes you valuable in any company with an open-source security stack.
slides.sub: The `weird.log` is Zeek's anomaly detector. It logs any protocol behaviour that deviates from the RFC specification — unexpected flag combinations, malformed packets, protocol version mismatches. Security professionals treat every entry in weird.log as a priority investigation item. An empty weird.log means nothing unusual happened; a populated one means something did.
slides.mistake: Trying to read Zeek's tab-separated log files directly in a text editor for large captures. Zeek logs for a busy 1-hour capture can be millions of lines. Use `zeek-cut` to extract specific fields, pipe to `sort | uniq -c | sort -rn` for frequency analysis, and use `awk` for filtering. The Linux text processing tools from Phase 3 apply directly here.

cover.title: Network Forensics __Investigation Workflow__
cover.hook: A breach happened. The pcap is your crime scene. Here is the full investigation workflow.

slides.label: Investigation Framework
slides.heading: Investigation Framework: __5 Steps__

body.text: Every professional network forensics investigation follows a consistent framework regardless of the incident type. Skipping steps leads to missed evidence, incorrect conclusions, and incomplete reports.
**Step 1 — Define scope:** What timeframe? Which systems? Which network segments? Narrow scope = faster analysis and more precise findings.
**Step 2 — Profile normal traffic:** What does baseline look like? Protocol distribution, top talkers, connection patterns. Anomalies only exist relative to normal.
**Step 3 — Identify anomalies:** Deviations from baseline. Unknown external IPs, unusual protocols, unexpected ports, traffic spikes.
**Step 4 — Reconstruct attack timeline:** First contact → exploitation → C2 → lateral movement → exfiltration. Minute-by-minute narrative.
**Step 5 — Extract IOCs:** All C2 IPs, domains, file hashes, URLs. Feed into threat intel for blocking and hunting.

slides.time: 25 min · Wireshark University · SANS Network Forensics

slides.label: Initial Triage
slides.heading: Initial Triage: __2 Minutes of Statistics__

body.text: Before applying any filters, spend exactly 2 minutes on statistics. This gives you a complete traffic overview and prevents the tunnel-vision of jumping straight to filtering.
**Statistics → Protocol Hierarchy:** What protocols are present? Any unusual ones (Tor, IRC, unusual tunnels)? What percentage is each protocol?
**Statistics → Conversations:** Top IP pairs by bytes. Any internal→external connections with massive data transfer? Potential exfiltration?
**Statistics → Endpoints:** All unique IPs. Any internal IPs you do not recognise? New hosts that appeared post-compromise?
**Statistics → I/O Graph:** Traffic spikes at specific times. Identify the exact moment the attack began — visible as a traffic pattern change.

slides.time: 20 min · Run full triage on 3 malware pcaps

slides.label: IOC Extraction
slides.heading: IOC Extraction: __Document the Evidence__

body.text:
# In Wireshark — export HTTP objects (malware downloads):
File → Export Objects → HTTP
# Hash all extracted files with sha256sum
# Check hashes against VirusTotal

# Extract unique external IPs from Zeek conn.log:
cat conn.log | zeek-cut id.resp_h \
  | sort | uniq > external_ips.txt
# Check each against VirusTotal, AbuseIPDB, Shodan

# Extract all DNS queries from Zeek dns.log:
cat dns.log | zeek-cut query \
  | sort | uniq > domains_queried.txt
# Check against VirusTotal domain intel
**Final IOC list format:** {type: 'ip', value: '45.33.32.156', source: 'conn.log', confidence: 'high'} — structured for import into MISP, OpenCTI, or your SIEM blocklist.

slides.time: 25 min · Extract and enrich IOCs from a malware pcap

slides.label: Incident Report Writing
slides.heading: Incident Report: __Communicate Your Findings__

body.text: **DFIR report structure** — same format used by every major incident response firm:
**Executive Summary:** 3–5 sentences. What happened, when, what was impacted, what was done. Non-technical management reads ONLY this.
**Timeline of Events:** Minute-by-minute attack narrative from first packet to last. Shows the attack progression.
**Technical Analysis:** Detailed findings with evidence — Wireshark screenshots, Zeek log excerpts, file hashes, PCAP references.
**IOC List:** All IPs, domains, file hashes, URLs, email addresses identified. Table format for easy import into security tools.
**Recommendations:** 3–5 specific, actionable remediation steps with priority levels.

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: Practise writing incident reports. The ability to clearly communicate technical findings to non-technical management is what separates $60k from $90k+ security analysts. Technical skill alone is not enough.
slides.sub: The Executive Summary is the most important section of any incident report — and the hardest to write. A CTO or CEO will read only those 5 sentences and make resource allocation decisions based on them. Write it last, after you understand the full picture. Every word must earn its place: what happened, who was affected, what was done, what remains.
slides.mistake: Writing technical incident reports that only other security analysts can understand. Real incident reports serve multiple audiences: the executive summary for leadership, the technical analysis for your peers, the IOC list for security tools. Each section must be written for its specific audience. A report that only technical people can understand fails its primary purpose.

cover.title: Python + Wireshark: __Parsing PCAPs__
cover.hook: Python meets Wireshark. Automate pcap analysis with Scapy and PyShark.

slides.label: Reading PCAPs with Scapy
slides.heading: Scapy: __Read Any PCAP__ Programmatically

body.text:
from scapy.all import rdpcap, IP, TCP, DNS, DNSQR

# Load entire pcap into memory:
packets = rdpcap('malware_capture.pcap')
print(f'Total packets: {len(packets)}')

# Print summary of each packet:
for pkt in packets:
    print(pkt.summary())

# Filter for specific layer types:
tcp_packets = [p for p in packets if p.haslayer(TCP)]
dns_packets = [p for p in packets if p.haslayer(DNS)]

print(f'TCP packets: {len(tcp_packets)}')
print(f'DNS packets: {len(dns_packets)}')

# Extract DNS queries (what domains were queried?):
for pkt in dns_packets:
    if pkt.haslayer(DNSQR):  # DNS query record
        domain = pkt[DNSQR].qname.decode('utf-8')
        print(f'DNS Query: {domain}')

slides.time: 25 min · scapy.net docs · realpython.com Scapy guide

slides.label: PyShark for App-Layer Analysis
slides.method: WHAT — PyShark Wireshark Dissectors in Python
slides.heading: PyShark: __Wireshark Dissectors__ in Python

body.text:
import pyshark
# pip install pyshark  (requires Wireshark/tshark installed)

# Load pcap with display filter:
cap = pyshark.FileCapture(
    'malware.pcap',
    display_filter='http'
)

for pkt in cap:
    try:
        src = pkt.ip.src
        host = pkt.http.host
        uri  = pkt.http.request_uri
        ua   = pkt.http.user_agent
        print(f'{src} → {host}{uri} [{ua}]')
    except AttributeError:
        pass  # Not all HTTP packets have all fields

**PyShark vs Scapy:** PyShark uses Wireshark's dissectors — it understands every protocol Wireshark understands. Scapy is faster for raw packet manipulation. PyShark is better for application-layer analysis where you need fields like `http.user_agent` or `dns.qry.name`.

slides.label: Automated PCAP Analyser
slides.method: HOW — Building an Automated Triage Script
slides.heading: Auto-Analyser: __30-Line Triage Script__

body.text:
import pyshark
from collections import Counter

blocklist = ['evil.com', 'malware.ru', 'c2-server.net']

def analyse_pcap(pcap_file):
    cap = pyshark.FileCapture(pcap_file,
                              display_filter='dns')
    dns_queries = []
    for pkt in cap:
        try:
            domain = pkt.dns.qry_name
            dns_queries.append(domain)
        except AttributeError:
            pass
    
    # Count and find suspicious domains:
    counter = Counter(dns_queries)
    print('\nTop 10 Queried Domains:')
    for domain, count in counter.most_common(10):
        flag = ' ⚠️ BLOCKLISTED' if domain in blocklist else ''
        print(f'  {domain}: {count}{flag}')

slides.sub: The combination of Zeek logs + Python analysis is extremely powerful. Zeek processes the raw pcap into structured TSV logs. Python reads those logs with pandas, applies analysis logic, enriches with threat intel APIs, and generates reports. This pipeline processes millions of packets in seconds — no GUI required, fully automatable, infinitely scalable.
slides.mistake: Processing large pcap files by loading them entirely into RAM with rdpcap(). A 1GB pcap will consume several GB of RAM when loaded into Scapy. For large files, use Scapy's PcapReader as an iterator: `with PcapReader('large.pcap') as reader: for pkt in reader:` — this processes one packet at a time and never loads the full file into memory.

slides.label: What is Security Onion?
slides.heading: Security Onion: __Free Enterprise SIEM__

body.text: **Security Onion** is a free, open-source Linux distribution for threat hunting, enterprise security monitoring, and log management. It bundles a complete security operations stack into a single ISO — eliminating the need to configure each tool individually.
**Detection:** Suricata IDS — alerts on known attack signatures with ATT&CK mapping
**Traffic Analysis:** Zeek NSM — structured logs for every network event
**Storage & Search:** Elasticsearch + Kibana — search billions of events instantly
**Forensics:** CyberChef built-in — decode, decrypt, analyse artefacts in browser
**Case Management:** Built-in ticketing for tracking incidents
**Cost:** $0. This exact stack is used in real enterprise SOC environments. 'Security Onion' on a resume is an immediate signal of operational experience.

**Create VM:** Minimum 4GB RAM (8GB recommended), 80GB disk, 2 CPUs. Add two NICs: one for management (NAT), one for monitoring (Host-Only or promiscuous mode).

body.text: **Alerts view:** Shows all Suricata detections with severity levels, source/destination IPs, ATT&CK technique mappings, and one-click pivot to the raw packet that triggered the alert. Every alert is a case you can track.

**Hunt module:** Query all data by IP, domain, hash, or any field across Zeek and Suricata logs simultaneously. No SIEM query language required — point-and-click pivoting between related evidence. Find all connections to a suspicious IP, all DNS queries for a domain, all files with a hash.

**PCAP import:** Security Onion supports direct pcap import through the interface. Upload any malware pcap → it processes through Zeek and Suricata automatically → all findings appear in Alerts and Hunt within minutes.

**ATT&CK Navigator view** shows your detection coverage as a heatmap overlaid on the full ATT&CK matrix. Green cells = you have detection for that technique. Empty cells = detection gaps where an attacker could operate undetected.

**Professional value:** Being able to say 'our Suricata ruleset covers 67% of ATT&CK techniques in the Initial Access and Execution tactics' is exactly the kind of metric security leadership wants to see. Security Onion makes this measurable.

cover.title: Network __Threat Hunting__ Techniques
cover.hook: Threat hunters do not wait for alerts. They go looking for hidden attackers. Here is how.

slides.label: What is Threat Hunting?
slides.heading: Threat Hunting: __Proactive, Not Reactive__

body.text: **Threat hunting** is a proactive, analyst-driven search for threats that have already bypassed automated detection. The core assumption is: 'We have already been breached somewhere — where is the attacker hiding?'

**Reactive vs Proactive:** A SOC analyst waits for alerts to come to them — that is reactive. A threat hunter generates hypotheses, collects data, and searches for evidence — that is proactive. Same skills, different mindset.

**Why it exists:** Advanced attackers specifically design their tools and techniques to evade automated detection. Signature-based IDS misses what it has never seen. A skilled human analyst hunting with the right data catches what automation cannot.

slides.time: 20 min · SANS Threat Hunting free intro · attack.mitre.org

slides.label: Hypothesis-Based Hunting
slides.heading: Hypothesis Hunting: __ATT&CK → Evidence__

body.text: **The structured process:** MITRE ATT&CK technique → form a hypothesis → identify what network evidence would confirm it → collect that data → analyse → confirm or deny. Structured curiosity, not random searching.
**Technique:** T1071 (Application Layer Protocol) — adversaries use standard protocols (HTTP, DNS, SMTP) for C2 to blend in with normal traffic.
**Hypothesis:** 'Is there malware using HTTP beaconing to a C2 server, blending in with normal web traffic?'
**Evidence to collect:** HTTP connections to unusual destinations at regular intervals, unusual User-Agent strings, small regular HTTP POST requests.
**Filter to apply:** `http.request.method==POST && http.content_length < 100` — small regular POSTs from internal hosts to external IPs.
**Result:** Either you confirm the hypothesis (found the threat) or deny it (confirmed that specific technique is not present) — both are valuable outcomes.

slides.time: 25 min · attack.mitre.org — explore the Lateral Movement tactic

slides.label: Baseline Deviation Hunting
slides.heading: Baseline Deviation: __Spot What Changed__

body.text: **Know normal to spot abnormal.** Establish baselines for your environment — normal DNS query rate per host, normal number of external connections, normal protocols in use, normal working hours traffic patterns.

# In Zeek conn.log analysis with Python/Pandas:
# Normal: each workstation makes ~50 external connections/hour
# Anomaly: one workstation making 5000/hour
# = possible beaconing, DGA, or exfiltration in progress

# DNS baseline example:
# Normal: 5 DNS queries per minute per host
# Anomaly: 500 queries per minute = DGA malware or DNS tunnel
# Action: filter dns.flags.rcode==3 for that host
#         — look for NXDomain flood pattern
**Statistical threshold:** Flag any value beyond 2 standard deviations from the mean as worthy of investigation. Not every anomaly is malicious — but every attack IS anomalous relative to baseline.

slides.time: 20 min · Establish and document baselines from your own captures

slides.label: Long-Tail Analysis
slides.heading: Long-Tail Analysis: __Hunt in the Rare Events__

body.text: **The long-tail principle:** In any large dataset, the most common events are almost certainly benign — thousands of hosts make the same DNS queries, visit the same websites, use the same applications. The rare, unique events are where attackers hide.

# Long-tail analysis with Zeek + Linux tools:
# Find LEAST common external IPs (attackers hide here):
cat conn.log | zeek-cut id.resp_h \
  | sort | uniq -c | sort -n | head -20
# The IPs at the TOP (smallest count) = investigate first

# Rarest User-Agents (malware blends in poorly):
cat http.log | zeek-cut user_agent \
  | sort | uniq -c | sort -n | head -10
# Agents appearing only once = immediate investigation

**Why it works:** Your standard browser connects to the same CDNs, ad networks, and analytics services as thousands of other machines — generating massive frequency counts. Malware C2 traffic is unique — appears only in the rarest events.

slides.time: 20 min · Run long-tail analysis on a Zeek conn.log

slides.heading: Day 55 Checklist

Identify **what network evidence** would confirm or deny each hypothesis
Write a **Wireshark filter or Zeek query** for each hypothesis
Run **long-tail analysis** on a Zeek conn.log — identify the rarest external IPs and User-Agents
Understand the **PEAK framework** (Prepare, Execute, Act, Knowledge) — the interview answer for hunting methodology

slides.heading: Your Hands-On __Task__ for Day 55

**Write** a hunting hypothesis for each: 'Is [technique] present in my network traffic?'

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: Threat hunting roles pay 20–40% more than reactive SOC analyst roles. Same skills, applied proactively instead of reactively. The career ceiling is dramatically higher.
slides.sub: The PEAK framework is the interview answer for 'explain your hunting methodology': Prepare (define scope, collect data, identify data sources) → Execute (run the hunt, analyse data) → Act on findings (document, escalate, remediate, create detections from confirmed threats) → Knowledge (update baselines, share findings with team). Four words that demonstrate operational maturity.
slides.mistake: Hunting without a documented hypothesis. Random data exploration occasionally finds threats by luck — but it is not repeatable, not scalable, and cannot be taught. Hypothesis-driven hunting produces documented, reproducible processes that can be refined over time. If you cannot write down your hypothesis before starting, you are not hunting — you are browsing.

**Threat Hunter:** Proactive ATT&CK-based hunting as a full-time role. Uses everything from Day 55 daily. Senior role, $90–130k+ range.
